# hot-tub-controller — operational notes

Working notes that complement the README and inline YAML comments. Keep this short and load-bearing — anything that can be derived from reading the code goes in comments, not here.

## Power architecture (load-bearing fact)

**The ESP32 is parasitically powered from the Balboa GS501Z J2 pin 1 (+5 V).** It is NOT on a USB wall-wart or any separate PSU. The 5 V rail is generated by a small switching converter inside the spa controller, sized for the panel display (~50–100 mA). The ESP draws ~80–100 mA idle plus 200–500 mA peak during WiFi association — i.e. 2–5× the SMPS's design rating during transients.

Implication: brownouts during WiFi spikes are happening **inside the spa controller's SMPS**, not in any external PSU we can see. The ESP32's on-chip brownout detector (sdkconfig `CONFIG_ESP_BROWNOUT_DET_LVL_SEL_0` = 2.43 V) fires when this internal rail sags below 2.43 V.

Cabinet topology relevant context:
- The supply contactor (40 A Schneider Acti9) is driven from a Shelly **in the consumer unit**, several metres from the cabinet
- The planned heater Shelly was never fitted
- There is no permanent live in the cabinet today — every wire there is on the switched side

## Brownout mitigation — staged plan

Each step is independently useful. Skip later steps if earlier ones are enough. Order matters because steps 2–4 unlock progressively more headroom; flashing patches earlier without step 2 risks the ESP-IDF rollback loop documented in `.claude/morning-report-20260628.md`.

### Step 1 — firmware-level WiFi current trimming (DONE, in working tree)

Already in `esphome/hot-tub.yaml`:

- `wifi: output_power: 14dB` — drops association-burst current ~250 mA → ~150 mA (bench RSSI is -49 dBm, comfortable margin at 14 dB)
- `wifi: fast_connect: true` — skips network scan on boot, ~3 s sooner to API
- `wifi: power_save_mode: light` — drops idle current ~80 mA → ~20 mA between transmits

To be considered before next flash (not yet in YAML):

- **Static IP** (skip DHCP DISCOVER on boot — needs pfSense static lease for 10.20.0.34 first)
- `power_save_mode: high` instead of `light` — adds 50–100 ms latency on incoming API messages but drops idle current to ~10 mA
- Bump sensor `update_interval: 30s → 60s` (heating-experiment doesn't need sub-minute resolution)
- Bump `wifi_signal` and `uptime` update intervals to 300s

Final TX-power tuning **deferred until the perfboard build is in place** — cabinet metalwork will swing RSSI 15–20 dB, makes any bench measurement non-representative.

### Step 2 — physical decoupling caps at the ESP VIN (NEXT — parts on order)

Math check for a typical WiFi TX burst (500 mA peak, ~3 ms, on a ~100 mA SMPS — cap must bridge ~400 mA × 3 ms = 1.2 mC):

| Capacitance | Voltage sag during the spike | Brownout survival |
|---|---|---|
| 1000 µF | 1.2 V | ❌ edge of LDO dropout |
| 2000 µF (2× 1000 µF in parallel) | 0.6 V | ✓ safe margin |
| 3000 µF | 0.4 V | ✓ diminishing returns past here |

Recommended BOM (~£1.20 total):

- **2× 1000 µF, 16 V, 105 °C, low-ESR** aluminium electrolytic (Panasonic FR / Rubycon ZLH / Nichicon HM)
- **2× 100 nF, 50 V, X7R** ceramic (any reputable brand)
- **1× 1 Ω, 0.5 W** through-hole resistor (only fit if ESP fails to start after caps installed — inrush limiter)
- **Optional polish**: 1× 100–220 µF solid polymer (Panasonic FP) replacing one of the electrolytics for better ESR

Layout — distribute, don't pile:

```
J2 connector  ──[opt 1Ω]──┬──────────────────────┬──── ESP VIN
                          │                      │
                       [1000µF]              [1000µF]
                       + 100nF               + 100nF
                          │                      │
J2 ground  ───────────────┴──────────────────────┴──── ESP GND
```

- ≤25 mm leads from each cap to its load point
- 100 nF ceramic as close to the 1000 µF electrolytic as physically possible (handles the HF edge the bulk cap can't)
- ALL components share the SAME ground node as J2 pin 4 — that's the mandatory signal reference for the display bus

### Step 3 — separate aux PSU, disconnect J2 +5 V

When the cabinet build is being finalised:

- Run a small 5 V USB PSU off the same circuit the tub is on (still switched, but properly sized)
- **Disconnect J2 pin 1 (+5 V) at the ESP end** — cap it / leave floating
- **Keep J2 pin 4 (GND) connected** to ESP GND — mandatory shared signal reference for the display bus + button outputs
- New PSU GND → ESP GND (same node as J2 pin 4)

Solves the spa-SMPS overload entirely. Still cycles with the contactor (no permanent live in cabinet today).

### Step 4 — full rewire, permanent live in cabinet (longer term)

Move the contactor into the cabinet, permanent live for the aux PSU. Notifiable Part P work; bundle with other cabinet electrical work. Eliminates dormant-cycle brownouts too — ESP stays continuously powered regardless of contactor state.

## Firmware change history (this session)

- `1eaba7d fix(decoder): suppress measured-temp publish during boot priming window` — boot/transient garbage frames could escape as real readings before the panel stabilised; 180 s publish gate after boot suppresses them
- (uncommitted, this PR/commit) `decode_temp` `+100` Fahrenheit hundreds-indicator branch removed — was inherited from upstream Balboa code; the GS501Z protocol has no such bit, and bus noise on `p1` bits 5+4 was causing spurious 100 °C readings several times per day at dormant power-on transients
- (uncommitted) `safe_mode: { num_attempts: 100, boot_is_good_after: 5s }` — relaxes ESP-IDF bootloader rollback so transient brownouts don't eat freshly-flashed firmware. **Note**: this does NOT disable `CONFIG_BOOTLOADER_APP_ROLLBACK_ENABLE`; a hard rollback can still occur if the new firmware dies within the first 5 s post-boot
- (uncommitted) WiFi current trimming (above)
- (uncommitted) Ambient `dallas_temp` block commented out — both DS18B20 entries were binding to the same single physical sensor (only one wired); re-enable when the second probe is on the bus and addresses are pinned

## Verification: which firmware is actually running on the device

The ESPHome OTA host-side "Update successful" message is *not* sufficient — ESP-IDF can silently roll back to the previous partition if the new firmware fails to mark valid in time. Always cross-check via the device's web server:

```bash
ssh -i ~/.ssh/ha_kilnside root@62.238.41.183 \
  "curl -s 'http://10.20.0.34/text_sensor/esphome_version'"
```

Returns the running firmware's compile timestamp + config hash. If the `built …` timestamp doesn't match what `esphome run` just reported, the OTA didn't actually replace the running firmware.

## Operationally — known automation interactions on the HA side

The firmware doesn't own the supply state. Three HA automations do:

- `hot_tub_supply_keepalive` (in `automations_yaml_only.yaml`)
- `hot_tub_supply_on_when_rate_cheap` / `_off_when_rate_expensive` (in `automations.yaml`)
- The dormant package `hot_tub_dormant_nightly_circulation` and `_temp_grab` (in `packages/hot_tub_dormant.yaml`)

All five now gate on `input_boolean.hot_tub_dormant_mode` to prevent the keep-alive-vs-dormant fight diagnosed on 2026-06-28. Don't touch this without understanding the gate semantics — see the comments in those HA YAML files.

## Heating experiment data quality

The DS18B20 water probe (one currently wired) is the trustworthy water-temp signal — independent of the display-bus decoder bugs. UA-fitting work in [[project-hot-tub-heating-experiment]] should use the `_probe` entity, not the `_water_temperature` entity, until decoder reliability is improved.

Tub physical parameters derived from the 2026-06-28 19:18→21:35 BST heating event:
- Heating rate: ~3.45 °C/h with 3 kW (heater + circ pump) and ~20 °C outdoor
- Volume: ~675 L (range 650–700 L depending on assumed UA)
- Heater element: ~2.6 kW (under 3 kW nameplate)
- Circulation pump: ~350 W in-line wet-mount (treat as 100 % water-coupled heat)
